name: Cumulative Frequency, Quartiles and Spread
whatIsIt: CUMULATIVE FREQUENCY is a running total — for each value (or class), you add up all the frequencies so far, answering 'how many are AT MOST this much?'. Plotting cumulative frequency gives an S-shaped OGIVE. From it we read the MEDIAN (the middle, at half the total), the LOWER and UPPER QUARTILES (at a quarter and three-quarters), and the INTERQUARTILE RANGE (IQR = upper quartile − lower quartile), which measures the spread of the middle half of the data.
whyNeeded: Cumulative frequency answers 'how many scored below the pass mark?' or 'what mark do the top 25% beat?'. Quartiles and IQR describe how spread out data is without being fooled by one extreme value — essential in exams (ICSE, IGCSE), science and any data report.
realLifeUses: Finding how many students scored below 50 in a test
The mark the top 25% of a class beat (upper quartile)
Comparing the spread of two classes' results fairly (IQR)
Percentile ranks on a growth chart or an entrance exam

vocabulary.meaning: The running total of frequencies up to and including a value.
The middle value; at half the total on the ogive.
Values at one-quarter (Q1) and three-quarters (Q3) of the data.
Q3 − Q1: the spread of the middle 50% of the data.

story.title: The Class Test
story.text: Sir has 20 test scores. Priya asks, 'How many scored 30 or less?' Sir adds the frequencies up to 30 — a running total — and says '8'. 'That's cumulative frequency!' Then he finds the score at position 10 (half of 20): the MEDIAN. The score a quarter of the way up is Q1, three-quarters up is Q3, and Q3 − Q1 tells him how spread out the middle of the class is. 'One running total,' smiles Priya, 'unlocks all of it.'
story.extractedProblem: In 20 scores, Q1 = 25 and Q3 = 45. What is the interquartile range?

visual.caption: Cumulative frequency: each bar is the running total 'at most this score'. The bars only rise, ending at the grand total (20). Joined by a smooth curve this is the ogive.

teachingGallery.title: Building the running total
teachingGallery.note: Add each new frequency to the total so far — it never goes down.

examples.caption: Ordinary frequency: how many in EACH class (3, 5, 6, 6).

standardMethod.summary: Make a cumulative-frequency column by adding each frequency to the running total. Plot cumulative frequency against the upper boundary of each class to get the ogive. Read the median at N/2, Q1 at N/4, Q3 at 3N/4, then IQR = Q3 − Q1.
steps: List classes in order and add each frequency to the running total (the last total = N, the number of items).
Plot cumulative frequency (up) against the upper class boundary (across) and join with a smooth curve — the ogive.
Read across from N/2 to the curve, then down, for the MEDIAN; from N/4 for Q1; from 3N/4 for Q3.
Compute the interquartile range: IQR = Q3 − Q1.

workedExamples.problem: Frequencies for four classes are 3, 5, 6, 6. Write the cumulative frequencies.
For a data set Q1 = 32 and Q3 = 58. Find the interquartile range.

commonMistakes.mistake: Letting a cumulative frequency value go DOWN as you move along.
commonMistakes.fix: Cumulative frequency is a RUNNING TOTAL — it can only stay the same or increase, never decrease, ending at N.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the quartiles (Q3 + Q1) to find the IQR.
commonMistakes.fix: IQR is a SUBTRACTION: IQR = Q3 − Q1 (the spread of the middle half).

easy.q: Frequencies 2, 4, 5. The last cumulative frequency (total) is?

medium.q: N = 40. The median is read at cumulative frequency…

challenge.q: N = 60. At what cumulative frequency do you read the upper quartile Q3?

questions.q: As you move along, cumulative frequency can…
N = 80. Median read at cumulative frequency?

teachBackPrompt: Take 10 test or game scores. Build a cumulative-frequency (running-total) table, then explain to a grown-up how you'd find the median at half the total and the IQR as Q3 − Q1 — and why the running total never goes down.

revisionCard.summary: Cumulative frequency = running total ('at most'). Plot vs upper boundary → ogive (S-curve, always rising, ends at N). Median at N/2, Q1 at N/4, Q3 at 3N/4. IQR = Q3 − Q1 = spread of the middle 50%.

meta.curriculum: ICSE / IGCSE / A-level (cumulative frequency, quartiles, IQR)

rememberIt.hook: Running total → ogive; Median N/2, Q1 N/4, Q3 3N/4; IQR = Q3 − Q1
rememberIt.unpack: Cumulative frequency only rises to N. Quartiles cut the data into four; IQR is the middle-half spread.

studentQuestions.q: Why use IQR instead of range?
studentQuestions.a: The full range can be ruined by one extreme value; the IQR looks only at the middle 50%, so it's a fairer measure of spread.
A box-and-whisker diagram drawn from the minimum, Q1, median, Q3 and maximum — a quick picture of spread built from these same quartiles.

formulas.name: Interquartile range
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring the spread of the middle half.

formulas.name: Reading positions
formulas.formula: Median at N/2, Q1 at N/4, Q3 at 3N/4
formulas.remember: half, quarter, three-quarters up the ogive
formulas.whenToUse: Reading quartiles off a cumulative-frequency curve.
